retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Alpha Thalassemia -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Thalassemia can be easily confused with iron deficiency anemia (IDA), which needs to be ruled out in a patient with low hemoglobin. Diagnostic tests like complete blood count (CBC) and hemoglobin analysis by high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) or electrophoresis may help identify the hemoglobin disorder in patients with chronic anemia (see Image . Hemoglobin Electrophoresis Patterns of Hemoglobin Disorders). [6] More advanced α-thalassemia mutation analytical techniques, such as allele-specific polymerase chain reaction (PCR), reverse dot blot (RDB) analysis, real-time PCR, and DNA sequencing, may also be useful for genetic counseling. [7] Disease management, parental counseling, antenatal diagnosis, newborn screening, and complication prevention are critical for improving the patients' and their families' quality of life.

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Alpha Thalassemia -- Diagnostic Tests
retrieved_docs.content: Hemoglobin analysis: HPLC or electrophoresis can quantify the different hemoglobin types in the patient’s blood (see Image . α-Thalassemia Major on Hemoglobin Electrophoresis). These techniques can also help differentiate different thalassemia types with good precision and reproducibility. [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Alpha Thalassemia -- Testing Procedures -- Capillary Electrophoresis System
retrieved_docs.content: Hemoglobin components are separated using silica capillaries in an alkaline buffer medium. Hemoglobin is negatively charged in alkaline solutions and thus migrates to the anode. The structural hemoglobin variants, having different surface charges, separate. The photometry technique then determines the amounts of each variant at a wavelength of 415 nm. Capillary electrophoresis can be used for both prenatal and postnatal hemoglobinopathy diagnosis. [29] Both HPLC and capillary electrophoresis can detect HbH, Hb Bart’s, and HbCS. [30]

retrieved_docs.title: Electrophoresis -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The electrophoresis apparatus shows abnormal hemoglobin electrophoretic patterns in various hemoglobinopathies and thalassemia (see Image. Hemoglobin Electrophoresis Patterns). Comparing abnormal fractions with the control sample helps clinicians narrow down the diagnosis of hemoglobinopathies and thalassemia. References for the table. [19] [20] The presence of abnormal bands or attenuation of the normal band in serum protein electrophoresis can provide clinicians with valuable insight into the ongoing disease process. Reference for the table. [21] References for the table. [21] [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Beta Thalassemia -- Interfering Factors
retrieved_docs.content: In laboratories, the buffers, agents, and stains, if stored improperly, can yield false results and proper slide fixation and staining. Hemoglobin electrophoresis and HPLC can detect other hemoglobins like HbS, Hb C, Hb E, and Hb O, which can interact with beta thalassemia. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Alpha Thalassemia -- Testing Procedures -- Reverse Dot Blot Analysis
retrieved_docs.content: A healthy individual normally shows positive dots with each wild-type probe but not with any of the mutant probes. Meanwhile, heterozygotes often have a mixed pattern, demonstrating hybridization with both mutant and normal dots. Homozygotes typically demonstrate hybridization with the mutant probe only and not with normal probes.

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Alpha Thalassemia -- Diagnostic Tests
retrieved_docs.content: Molecular testing: allele-specific PCR, RDB analysis, gap-PCR, real-time PCR with melting curve analysis, and DNA sequencing can help identify the mutation causing α-thalassemia. Molecular testing is essential to genetic counseling and therapeutic research. [24] [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Beta Thalassemia -- Testing Procedures
retrieved_docs.content: For qualitative and quantitative hemoglobin analysis, there are various procedures available. The most widely used among them are electrophoresis and high-performance liquid chromatography.

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Alpha Thalassemia -- Results, Reporting, and Critical Findings -- α-Thalassemia Major (Hb Bart's Hydrops Fetalis Syndrome)
retrieved_docs.content: HPLC of a cordocentesis blood sample shows 1 or 2 very sharp and narrow peaks at the chromatogram injection point. [43] The major band is Hb Bart's, while the smaller band is attributable to Hb Portland. The complete absence of HbF should be noted. Alkaline electrophoresis shows the Hb Bart's band migrating to the anodal position and the Hb Portland band moving to the HbA position. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Beta Thalassemia -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Laboratory findings suggestive of thalassemia include microcytic hypochromic anemia. There may be significant anisopoikilocytosis (variation of size and shape) in cases of beta-thalassemia major on peripheral smear. Exclusion of iron deficiency and hemoglobin electrophoresis or high-performance liquid chromatography are often required for diagnosis. Treatment, if required, is primarily with blood transfusion, depending on the degree of anemia. Complications of beta-thalassemia include iron overload and bone-deforming marrow expansion with extramedullary hematopoiesis.

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Alpha Thalassemia -- Testing Procedures -- Reverse Dot Blot Analysis
retrieved_docs.content: The PCR product may be transferred on a membrane filter sheet as a dot to identify a suspected mutation more easily. This PCR product can then hybridize with an allele-specific oligomeric DNA probe. The probe can be radiolabeled with phosphorus for autoradiography or other reporter groups like biotin. Alternatively, an enzyme such as horseradish peroxidase may be attached. The product may be visualized using a chemiluminescent or colorimetric reaction.

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Beta Thalassemia -- Diagnostic Tests -- Molecular Testing
retrieved_docs.content: About 200 mutations causing beta thalassemia have been reported, and the most common among them is point mutation. [5] Point mutations are single-base substitutions, minor insertions, or deletions. Some commonly used DNA analysis techniques are Gap-PCR, reverse dot blot (RDB) analysis, real-time PCR with melting curve analysis, and DNA sequencing. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Alpha Thalassemia -- Testing Procedures -- Direct DNA Sequencing
retrieved_docs.content: PCR product sequencing can help in identifying the specific gene mutations in α-thalassemia. Sanger’s dideoxy termination method is the most widely used. [33]

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Alpha Thalassemia -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: α-thalassemia is a relatively common monogenic blood disorder found worldwide. α-globin production is regulated by 4 α genes located on chromosome 16. [3] α-thalassemia is usually caused by the affected allele's reduced (designated as α+) or complete absence (designated as α°) of globin chain production. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Alpha Thalassemia -- Testing Procedures -- High-Performance Liquid Chromatography
retrieved_docs.content: HPLC separates, identifies, and quantifies different hemoglobin molecules. The specimen is passed through a slightly negatively charged silica gel column. Highly positively charged hemoglobin variants have more affinity for the column and thus elute after less positively charged hemoglobin types. Inorganic phosphate buffers help during elution. [26] The chromatograms generated by HPLC show the retention time, peak, and percentage of different hemoglobin fractions, allowing for the accurate diagnosis of hemoglobinopathies and thalassemia. [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Beta Thalassemia -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Beta thalassemia is a hereditary hematological disorder with defects in beta globin chain synthesis. Based on the underlying molecular defect, they can have variable phenotypes ranging from asymptomatic carriers to severe transfusion-dependent anemia. The laboratory evaluation is based on various hematological tests like complete blood count, iron studies, peripheral smear, hemoglobin analysis, and molecular testing. The most reliable method for diagnosis is hemoglobin analysis, for which various techniques like high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), electrophoresis, and chromatography are available. Molecular diagnoses are needed in cases of severe transfusion-dependent and mild-moderate non-transfusion-dependent cases. DNA evaluation by chorionic villi sampling or culture of the fetal cell is used for a prenatal diagnosis. As it is a recessive disease, recognizing carriers can help prevent severe disease.

Overall, the interrelationships among chronic kidney disease–related anemia, various deficiency and hemolytic anemias, thalassemia, and related immune‑mediated conditions underscore the importance of integrated diagnostic and therapeutic strategies that address both hematologic abnormalities and their associated systemic manifestations.

retrieved_docs.content: stage. Recognizing these interconnections, the associated syndromes, and the relevant laboratory parameters (e.g., total hemoglobin) is essential for accurate diagnosis, interpretation of test results, and targeted management of infants during the neonatal and early infancy periods.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2774
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia is classified as داء الكريات المنجلية, the Arabic term for sickle cell disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2605
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell retinopathy (اعتلال الشبكية بالخلايا المنجلية) is clinically associated with thalassemia (الثلاسيميا). Both disorders are hemoglobinopathies that can coexist in the same patient, and the presence of thalassemia may influence the ocular manifestations observed in sickle cell disease. Recognizing this association underscores the importance of comprehensive ophthalmic assessment and coordinated care for individuals affected by either or both conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1802
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia is classified as داء الكريات المنجلية, and this hemoglobin disorder commonly manifests as نقص الأكسجين في الدم, indicating reduced oxygen levels in the blood.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 133
retrieved_docs.content: is noted for lacking an autoimmune mechanism despite its inclusion in the broader immune‑disorder framework. Thalassemia is also associated clinically with sickle‑cell retinopathy (اعتلال الشبكية بالخلايا المنجلية). Collectively, these entities illustrate that various deficiency and hemolytic anemias, especially those within the “other deficiency anemia” group, share immunologic associations and frequently intersect with chronic kidney disease and other immune‑mediated conditions, highlighting the need for coordinated hematologic, immunologic, and nephrologic assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1751
retrieved_docs.content: Factor VIII may be used to treat coagulation and hemorrhagic disorders, as it helps correct deficiencies in the clotting cascade that underlie these bleeding conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 4 -- Community 2812
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia, which is classified as داء الكريات المنجلية, is a hereditary hemoglobin disorder that can precipitate serious respiratory complications in adults. Clinically, داء الكريات المنجلية is associated with respiratory failure, respiratory insufficiency, and adult cardiac arrest, underscoring the need for vigilant respiratory monitoring and prompt intervention in patients affected by this disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2762
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia is classified as داء الكريات المنجلية, and داء الكريات المنجلية is clinically associated with قساح.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 4 -- Community 2884
retrieved_docs.content: The neonatal disorder (اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة) includes as a specific manifestation the condition of fetal erythroblastosis (كثرة الأرومات الحمر الجنينية), indicating that fetal erythroblastosis is a particular subtype or child entity within the broader category of neonatal disorders. Recognizing this relationship is important for accurate diagnosis and targeted management of affected newborns.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 198
retrieved_docs.content: outcomes, fetal growth disturbances, a spectrum of neonatal infections, genetic syndromes, bleeding events, various forms of jaundice, and traumatic injuries, providing a comprehensive framework for perinatal assessment, diagnosis, and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 1 -- Community 165
retrieved_docs.content: bleeding risk, infection, growth restriction, and traumatic delivery in newborn care. Laboratory evaluation, particularly measurement of total hemoglobin concentration, is interpreted against these interrelated conditions to guide diagnosis, risk stratification, and therapeutic decision‑making.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2004
retrieved_docs.content: The term “Abnormal” is used as an interpretation of فقر دم فانكوني (Fanconi anemia), indicating that the clinical and laboratory findings associated with this genetic disorder are considered abnormal. Fanconi anemia is a hereditary condition characterized by bone‑marrow failure, congenital anomalies, and a heightened risk of malignancies, and the descriptor “Abnormal” reflects the pathological nature of its presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2441
retrieved_docs.content: The term “Abnormal” is used as an interpretation for both the Arabic phrase “البزل السّلوي غير الطبيعي” and the condition “فقر دم فانكوني.” In clinical practice, describing a spinal tap as “البزل السّلوي غير الطبيعي” indicates an abnormal cerebrospinal fluid result, while labeling “فقر دم فانكوني” as abnormal reflects the presence of Fanconi anemia, a hereditary bone‑marrow failure syndrome. Recognizing that “Abnormal” serves as a common descriptor for these distinct entities highlights its role as a generic marker of pathological or atypical findings in patient evaluation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 1 -- Community 185
retrieved_docs.content: (نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ). Laboratory findings that fall below reference ranges, particularly low total hemoglobin concentration, are interpreted as indicative of hemolytic or perinatal jaundice, underscoring the diagnostic importance of quantitative testing in this age group. Collectively, these relationships illustrate that the neonatal period serves as a critical temporal nexus linking infectious, metabolic, hematologic, and hemorrhagic conditions—many organized under “other perinatal conditions” or “hemolytic‑jaundice” categories—and that recognizing these interconnections is essential for accurate assessment, diagnosis, and management of newborns.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1494
retrieved_docs.content: A gestational age of 29‑30 weeks completed is associated with a liveborn infant, indicating that pregnancies reaching this stage can result in a live birth.
